definition: A binary search tree is a binary tree where each node keeps smaller values in its left subtree and larger values in its right subtree.
deepExplanation: A BST turns comparison into direction. At each node, compare the target value with the current node's value. If the target is smaller, move left; if it is larger, move right. When the tree is balanced, each comparison discards a large part of the remaining tree, so search, insert, and delete can be O(log n). If values are inserted in sorted order without balancing, the tree can stretch into a chain and operations become O(n).
whyItMatters: BSTs connect tree structure, recursion, searching, and Big-O tradeoffs in one visual model.
analogy: A BST is like a guessing game where each answer tells you which half of the remaining possibilities to ignore, but only if the choices are well balanced.

codeExamples.title: Iterative BST search

walkthrough.text: Each node stores a value and references to left and right children.
Search starts at the root and uses current to walk down the tree.
If the target matches the current node, the search succeeds immediately.
A smaller target moves left; a larger target moves right, following the BST ordering rule.
Reaching null means the target is not in the tree.

codeExamples.title: Recursive insert outline

walkthrough.text: A null position is the correct place to attach the new value.
The value chooses left or right using the same comparison rule as search.
Returning node reconnects the subtree after insertion.

diagrams.title: BST Ordering Rule
diagrams.caption: Every left subtree value is smaller than its parent, and every right subtree value is larger.

diagrams.title: Search for 6
diagrams.caption: BST search follows comparisons from the root until it finds the value or reaches null.

commonMistakes.mistake: Assuming every BST operation is always O(log n).
commonMistakes.whyItHappens: Balanced tree diagrams are common in examples.
commonMistakes.correction: State the condition: operations are O(log n) when the tree height is logarithmic; worst case is O(n).

commonMistakes.whyItHappens: Duplicate handling is often omitted from beginner examples.
commonMistakes.correction: Choose a duplicate policy, such as count duplicates or always reject them, and apply it consistently.

commonMistakes.mistake: Deleting a node with two children without preserving order.
commonMistakes.whyItHappens: Two-child deletion requires replacing the value with an in-order predecessor or successor.
commonMistakes.correction: Use the next larger value from the right subtree or next smaller value from the left subtree.

bestPractices: Track the tree height when discussing performance.
Test search paths that go left, right, and all the way to null.
Use balanced-tree variants when consistently logarithmic operations are required.

miniQuiz.question: In a BST with root 8 and left child 3, where does value 6 go relative to 3?
miniQuiz.explanation: 6 is smaller than 8, so it goes left from 8. It is larger than 3, so it goes right from 3.

miniQuiz.question: Why can sorted insertion make an unbalanced BST slow?
options: It can make the tree behave like a linked list
miniQuiz.answer: It can make the tree behave like a linked list
miniQuiz.explanation: If every new value goes to one side, the height becomes n, so search can require walking through many nodes.

flashcards.front: What is the BST ordering rule?
flashcards.back: Smaller values go in the left subtree; larger values go in the right subtree.

flashcards.front: When is BST search O(log n)?
flashcards.back: When the tree is balanced enough that its height grows logarithmically with the number of nodes.

flashcards.front: What is the worst-case search time for an unbalanced BST?
flashcards.back: O(n), because the tree can degrade into a chain.
